mappings.rationale: Section A.1 covers ancient Greek and Roman philosophy from the Pre-Socratics through Hellenistic philosophy, which is the direct subject of this VSI.
Section A.1.a covers the Pre-Socratic philosophers including cosmology, metaphysics of matter, theory of knowledge, and Pythagorean metaphysics of number.
Section A.1.b.i covers the ethical concerns and positions of Socrates, the Socratic method, and his influence on subsequent philosophy.
Section A.1.b.ii covers Plato's dialogues on politics, ethics, metaphysics, epistemology, cosmology, and the relation of mathematics to philosophy.
Section A.1.b.iii covers Aristotle's metaphysics, logic, epistemology, biology, ethics, politics, and empirical researches.
Section A.1.c.i covers Stoic philosophy from Zeno through Chrysippus and its role during the late Roman Republic and empire.
Section A.1.c.ii covers the philosophy of the Epicureans including Epicurus's teaching about the universe, pleasure, and the gods.
Section A.1.c.iii covers the Skeptics including Pyrrhon's teaching about uncertainty in knowing.
Section A.2.a.i covers Augustine's use of Neoplatonist thought in his theology and his doctrine of man.
Section A.2.a.i specifically mentions Anselm's proofs of the existence of God as part of early medieval philosophy.
Section A.2.c covers Aquinas's synthesis of Aristotelianism and Christian theology as the height of Scholastic philosophy.
Section A.2.b covers the contribution of Arabic philosophy and the role of Islamic philosophers in transmitting Aristotle to the West.
Section A.2 covers philosophy in the Middle Ages from the patristic period through Scholasticism and late medieval thought.
Section A.2.b covers the contribution of Arabic and Jewish philosophy and section C.6 covers Islamic philosophy as philosophy associated with religion.
Section A.3.a.i specifically names Machiavelli's views on the nature and moral status of political power in Renaissance philosophy.
Hobbes is named in sections A.3.a.i (political theory) and A.3.b.i (Empiricist tradition and theory of knowledge).
Section A.3.b.ii covers Descartes's antiempirical metaphysics and the dualism of his doctrine of man and the world.
Section A.3.b.ii names Spinoza as part of the Rationalist tradition providing speculative systems of philosophy.
Section A.3.b.ii names Leibniz as part of the Rationalist tradition providing speculative systems of philosophy.
Section A.3.c covers Locke's inquiry into the origin and nature of reason, and A.3.c.iii covers his concern with freedom and equality.
Section A.3.c.i covers Hume's science of man as part of Enlightenment epistemological issues.
Section A.3.c.i covers Kant's critical examination of reason during the Enlightenment.
Section A.3.c.iii covers Rousseau's concern with the freedom and equality of citizens in social and political philosophy.
Section A.3.d.i covers the Idealism of Hegel as part of the resurgence of the metaphysical spirit in 19th-century philosophy.
Section A.3.d.ii covers the dialectical Materialism of Marx and Engels, and A.3.e.ii covers developments in Marxist thought.
Section A.3.d.iii covers Kierkegaard's preoccupation with states of consciousness as a reaction against Rationalism.
Section A.3.d.iii covers Schopenhauer's doctrine of cosmic will as a reaction against Rationalism.
Section A.3.d.iii specifically mentions the writings of Nietzsche as part of the 19th-century reaction against Rationalism.
Section A.3.d.ii covers J.S. Mill's theory of knowledge and ethics as part of the empirical and scientific tradition.
Wittgenstein is a central figure in 20th-century philosophy, relevant to the developments covered in section A.3.e.
Russell is a major 20th-century philosopher relevant to the developments in logic, epistemology, and analytic philosophy covered in section A.3.e.
Heidegger is a major 20th-century philosopher whose existential and phenomenological thought is relevant to section A.3.e.
Arendt is a major 20th-century social and political philosopher relevant to the independent speculative and social philosophies in section A.3.e.i.
Adorno is a major 20th-century critical theorist relevant to the independent social philosophies discussed in section A.3.e.i.
Simone Weil is a 20th-century philosopher and mystic whose thought engages with ethics, politics, and metaphysics covered in section A.3.e.
Habermas is a major 20th-century social philosopher relevant to section A.3.e.i on independent speculative and social philosophies.
Foucault is a major 20th-century philosopher relevant to independent social philosophies and developments discussed in section A.3.e.
Derrida is a major 20th-century philosopher relevant to developments in Continental philosophy covered in section A.3.e.
Barthes's structuralist and post-structuralist thought is relevant to 20th-century philosophical developments in section A.3.e.
Section B.1 covers Indian philosophy from early Hindu and Buddhist thought through the schools of Vedanta and later developments.
Section B.2.a covers Confucianism as a classical Chinese philosophical school, and B.2.c covers Neo-Confucianism.
Section B.1.a covers Buddhist sacred literature in presystematic philosophy, and sections C.2 and B.3.a cover Buddhist philosophy.
The Buddha's teachings are foundational to Buddhist philosophy discussed in sections B.1.a (Indian context) and B.3.a (Japanese context).
Section B.1 covers Hindu philosophical thought including Vedanta schools and Vaisnava and Saiva schools, and C.1 covers Hindu philosophy.
Tibetan Buddhism represents a tradition within Buddhist philosophy covered in sections B.1 and C.2.
Continental philosophy covers major traditions from phenomenology through poststructuralism relevant to 20th-century developments in section A.3.e.
German philosophy spans the Rationalist tradition (A.3.b), Idealism (A.3.d.i), and 20th-century developments (A.3.e).
French philosophy covers developments from the Enlightenment (A.3.c) through 20th-century movements like existentialism and structuralism.
Analytic philosophy is a major 20th-century tradition relevant to section A.3.e, encompassing logical analysis and linguistic philosophy.
Existentialism's roots in Kierkegaard (A.3.d.iii) and its 20th-century development are covered in this section's historical trajectory.
Section B.2.d covers the effects of Maoism on 20th-century Chinese philosophy.
Voltaire is a major Enlightenment thinker relevant to section A.3.c on philosophy in the Age of Reason.
Tocqueville's political philosophy is relevant to the social and political philosophy discussed in section A.3.c.iii.
Provides an overview of the discipline whose history is the subject of this entire section.
Philosophical method has developed throughout the history of philosophy, from Socratic method (A.1.b.i) through modern analytic approaches.
Section A.1.c.v covers Jewish and Christian philosophy during the Hellenistic Age, and A.2.a covers the patristic period.
Section A.3.c.iii covers developments in religious philosophy, and A.3.c.ii covers the effect of scientific discoveries on philosophical thought.
Ethical thought is a major thread throughout the history of philosophy from Socrates (A.1.b.i) through J.S. Mill (A.3.d.ii).
Buddhist ethics relates to the ethical and political thought discussed in sections B.1.b and C.2.
Montaigne exemplifies Renaissance humanism (A.3.a.ii) with renewed interest in ancient Skepticism and Stoicism.
Adam Smith's philosophical contributions to moral philosophy and political economy relate to Enlightenment social philosophy (A.3.c.iii).
Feminist philosophy is a significant 20th-century development relevant to the independent social philosophies covered in section A.3.e.i.
Maimonides was a major philosopher whose rationalist methodology influenced the history of philosophy, relevant to this section's coverage of medieval philosophy.
